---
title: "Coding & Collaboration at emLab"
subtitle: "The updated SOP: formatters, branch-based development, and code review"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: simple
    slide-number: true
    transition: slide
    background-transition: fade
    incremental: false
    code-line-numbers: false
    self-contained: true
---

```{=html}
<style>
.new-badge {
  background: #2aa198;
  color: #fff;
  font-size: 0.42em;
  padding: 0.12em 0.55em;
  border-radius: 0.4em;
  vertical-align: middle;
  margin-left: 0.45em;
  font-weight: 700;
  letter-spacing: 0.06em;
}
.reveal a { color: #2aa198; }
.reveal a:hover { color: #1f7f78; }
.semi-transparent { opacity: 0.4; }
.no-bullets, .no-bullets li {
  list-style: none !important;
  margin-left: 0 !important;
  padding-left: 0 !important;
}
.no-bullets li::before, .no-bullets li::marker { content: none !important; }
.reveal .small { font-size: 0.75em; }
.reveal table { font-size: 0.7em; }
</style>
```

## Why this matters

::: {.notes}
Frame the whole talk: code at emLab informs real decisions, so errors carry real costs. Two goals drive everything else: accuracy and reproducibility.
:::

Our analyses inform real policy decisions. A coding error can undermine our science and erode partner trust.

Everything we write serves two goals:

::: {.incremental}
- **Accuracy** — the code does what we think it does.
- **Reproducibility** — others (or future you) can re-run it and get the same result.
:::

## Requirements vs. best practices

::: {.notes}
Clarify the two tiers. Universal requirements are the shared baseline everyone follows. Best practices are what we aspire to, adapted per project.
:::

The SOP distinguishes two tiers:

::: {.incremental}
- **Universal requirements** — the baseline *everyone* follows, on *every* project.
- **Best practices** — what we aspire to, adapted per project.
:::

This talk focuses on the requirements — especially what's **new**.

## The universal requirements

::: {.notes}
GitHub is the platform for all coding work. These two are the foundation; the next slide covers the three that are new.
:::

GitHub is emLab's required platform for all coding work:

::: {.incremental}
- All code lives in a repo under [emlab-ucsb](https://github.com/emlab-ucsb) — README + logical structure.
- Every change tracked with Git and clear commit messages.
:::

## [[NEW]]{.new-badge} Additions {auto-animate="true"}

::: {.notes}
The single most important slide for an audience that knows the old SOP. Previously, "advanced" workflows varied project to project. Now three things are universal requirements.
:::

Previously, branches and pull requests *"varied from project to project."*

**Now three things are universal:**

::: {.incremental}
1. [Code formatters](../03-code/code-styling.qmd) — Air (R), Ruff (Python), on save.
2. [Branch-based development](../04-gh/05-branches.qmd) — never commit to `main`.
3. [Code review](../03-code/code-review.qmd) — via pull requests, planned up front.
:::

# 1 · Code formatters {background-color="#2aa198"}

## Style is documentation

::: {.notes}
Set up the philosophy before the tooling. Consistent style means diffs reflect real changes, linters catch bugs, and new contributors can read unfamiliar code.
:::

Where projects span years and many people, **style is documentation.**

Consistent style means:

::: {.incremental}
- diffs reflect *real* changes, not whitespace
- linters catch common bugs before runtime
- newcomers can read unfamiliar code without decoding it first
:::

## Style guides — the short version

::: {.notes}
Don't read every rule. Point to the guides and hit the universal R requirements, since R is most common at the lab.
:::

We defer to established guides per language: [Tidyverse](https://style.tidyverse.org/) (R), [PEP 8](https://peps.python.org/pep-0008/) (Python), [Google Style Guides](https://google.github.io/styleguide/) (others).

For R, the universal requirements:

::: {.small}
- `snake_case` for names · spaces after commas and around operators
- native pipe `|>` or `%>%` · 2-space indents, never tabs · ~80-char lines
:::

::: {.fragment}
The good news: **you don't memorize these — a formatter applies them for you.**
:::

## Formatters are required [[NEW]]{.new-badge}

::: {.notes}
This is the key new requirement in the Code chapter. A formatter rewrites layout automatically to match the style guide. We've adopted fast Rust-based tools that run on save.
:::

A **code formatter** automatically adjusts your code's layout — spacing, indentation, line breaks — to match the style guide. Instead of fixing every comma by hand, the formatter does it instantly.

::: {.callout-important}
To maintain consistency across emLab, formatters are **required** for all new code development.
:::

We've adopted fast, Rust-based tools that can run **automatically every time you save.**

## Air — required for R [[NEW]]{.new-badge}

::: {.notes}
Air is built by Posit and enforces Tidyverse style. Two steps: install the extension, enable format-on-save.
:::

**Air** is the required R formatter (built by Posit, enforces Tidyverse style).

1. In Positron / VS Code, open Extensions (`Cmd+Shift+X`) and install **"Air"** (by Posit).
2. Turn on format-on-save in your `settings.json`:

```json
{
  "[r]": {
    "editor.defaultFormatter": "posit.air",
    "editor.formatOnSave": true
  }
}
```

## Ruff — required for Python [[NEW]]{.new-badge}

::: {.notes}
Ruff is the formatter and linter for Python — extremely fast, replaces several older tools. Same pattern: install, enable on save.
:::

**Ruff** is the required Python formatter *and* linter (by Astral) — it replaces several older tools.

1. Install the **"Ruff"** extension from the marketplace.
2. Enable format-on-save:

```json
{
  "[python]": {
    "editor.defaultFormatter": "charliermarsh.ruff",
    "editor.formatOnSave": true,
    "editor.codeActionsOnSave": {
      "source.fixAll.ruff": "explicit",
      "source.organizeImports.ruff": "explicit"
    }
  }
}
```

## On RStudio

::: {.notes}
Air isn't tightly integrated in RStudio, but styling is still required. Mention the styler fallback and the "end files with a newline" setting.
:::

Styling is still required in RStudio, where Air is less integrated. Use the `styler` package:

::: {.incremental}
- `install.packages("styler")`, then **Addins → "Style active file"** before each commit.
- **Tools → Global Options → Code → Saving** → check *"Ensure that source files end with a newline."*
:::

::: {.aside}
Air can also be wired into RStudio as an external CLI formatter — see [Posit's instructions](https://posit-dev.github.io/air/editor-rstudio.html). Full detail: [Code styling](../03-code/code-styling.qmd).
:::

# 2 · Branch-based development {background-color="#2aa198"}

## The rule [[NEW]]{.new-badge}

::: {.notes}
State the requirement plainly. All development happens on a branch; we don't commit to main.
:::

> **All code development happens on a separate branch, so that we don't commit directly to `main`.**

::: {.incremental}
- Your changes stay **isolated** from the stable project until they're ready.
- A failed approach or a bug never touches `main` — abandon the branch and move on.
- Branches are what make **code review** possible.
:::

## What a branch is {auto-animate="true"}

::: {.notes}
Use the SOP's own diagram. A branch is a named pointer to a snapshot that then moves forward independently.
:::

Think of your history as a sequence of snapshots:

```
A --- B --- C --- D   (main)
```

A **branch** is a named pointer that starts here but moves forward independently:

```
A --- B --- C --- D           (main)
                  \
                   E --- F    (my-branch)
```

`main` is untouched until you explicitly merge.

## Why not commit to `main`?

::: {.notes}
Main is the stable, reviewed record — always runnable. Branch-based dev creates a checkpoint for review. Applies even solo. At emLab, a ruleset enforces it.
:::

`main` is the **stable, reviewed record** — it should always run.

::: {.incremental}
- Branching is a checkpoint: work → PR → review → *then* merge.
- Works **even solo** — review your own diff, build the habit.
- Many repos **block direct pushes to `main`** via a ruleset; if you hit that error, make a branch.
:::

## The workflow {auto-animate="true"}

::: {.notes}
Walk the six steps end to end. All of this also works through the GUI in Positron, VS Code, and RStudio.
:::

1. **Start from an up-to-date `main`** — `git switch main && git pull`
2. **Create a branch** — `git switch -c add-spatial-aggregation`
3. **Work and commit** in logical units
4. **Push** — `git push -u origin <branch>`
5. **Open a pull request** and merge
6. **Delete the branch** after merge

::: {.aside}
Every step also works through the Git panel / branch picker in Positron, VS Code, and RStudio — no terminal required.
:::

## Command reference

| Action | Command |
|---|---|
| Create & switch to a new branch | `git switch -c branch-name` |
| Switch to an existing branch | `git switch branch-name` |
| See all local branches | `git branch` |
| Push branch to GitHub | `git push -u origin branch-name` |
| Pull `main` into your branch | `git merge main` |
| Delete a local branch | `git branch -d branch-name` |

## Keep branches small and current

::: {.notes}
Two practical habits: merge main in periodically to keep conflicts small; keep branches focused and short-lived.
:::

::: {.incremental}
- **Stay current:** `git merge main` periodically — the longer a branch diverges, the harder the merge.
- **Stay focused:** one branch = one task. Short-lived branches are easy to review.
- Big task? Split it into smaller branches that build on each other.
:::

::: {.aside}
Full detail: [Working with branches](../04-gh/05-branches.qmd).
:::

# 3 · Pull requests & code review {background-color="#2aa198"}

## Pull requests [[NEW]]{.new-badge}

::: {.notes}
A PR is a request to merge one branch into another, and the standard venue for review. All changes to main come through a PR.
:::

A **pull request (PR)** proposes merging one branch into another — and is the standard venue for code review.

::: {.incremental}
- GitHub shows exactly which lines changed.
- Collaborators comment, ask questions, and approve.
- Creates a **permanent record** of *what* changed, *why*, and who reviewed it.
:::

**All changes to `main` come through a PR.**

## Opening a PR

::: {.notes}
The four fields. Emphasize a good title and description, linking issues, and draft PRs.
:::

Push your branch, then open a PR. In the form:

::: {.incremental}
- **Base:** the branch you merge *into* — usually `main`.
- **Compare:** your feature/analysis branch.
- **Title:** summarize the change, not a status.
- **Description:** what, why, what to focus on. Link issues ("Closes #42").
:::

::: {.fragment}
Not ready for review? Open a **Draft PR**.
:::

## Reviewing a PR {auto-animate="true"}

::: {.notes}
Three outcomes. Distinguish blocking concerns from suggestions.
:::

A reviewer evaluates both the **code** and the **analytical approach**. GitHub gives three outcomes:

::: {.incremental}
- **Comment** — feedback without approving or blocking.
- **Approve** — ready to merge.
- **Request changes** — specific issues must be addressed first.
:::

Use line-level comments for specifics; distinguish **blocking concerns** from **suggestions**.

## Plan code review up front [[NEW]]{.new-badge}

::: {.notes}
The biggest cultural shift. Code review is planned at project start, not reactive. Answer four questions. Budget 10-20% of coding time.
:::

The biggest shift: review is **planned at the start of a project**, not triggered by a journal requirement or a discovered error.

A plan answers four questions:

::: {.incremental}
- **What** gets reviewed · **when** · **by whom** · **to what standard**
:::

::: {.fragment}
Budget **10–20% of coding time**. Discuss it at [kickoff](../03-code/code-review.qmd).
:::

## Levels of review

::: {.notes}
Not every project needs the same review. Walk the three types and when each is required.
:::

| Review type | What it checks | When required |
|---|---|---|
| **Self** | Author reviews own code | Always / ongoing |
| **Accuracy** | Logic, calculations, correctness | Partner-facing & publications |
| **Reproducibility** | Reviewer independently re-runs it | Publications (gold standard) |

::: {.fragment}
Make a *conscious* decision about the right level — while timelines and budgets can still accommodate it.
:::

## Best practices {auto-animate="true"}

::: {.notes}
Split responsibilities. Authors make review easy; reviewers are specific and prioritize correctness.
:::

**If your code is being reviewed:**

::: {.incremental .small}
- Keep PRs **small and focused.** Write a clear description.
- Make sure it **runs end-to-end** before requesting review.
- Respond constructively — keep the discussion in the PR.
:::

## Best practices {auto-animate="true"}

**If you're reviewing:**

::: {.incremental .small}
- Know what kind of review is being asked for (accuracy? reproducibility? clarity?).
- Be **specific and constructive**; label "Required:" vs. "Nit:".
- **Prioritize correctness over style.** For reproducibility review, actually run the code.
- Be **timely** — As development occurs elsewhere on the project, a stale PR will become more likely to caues merge conflicts.
:::

## The culture matters

::: {.notes}
Close the section on culture. Review is non-adversarial, a contribution worth recognizing, and needs protected time.
:::

::: {.incremental}
- Review is **non-adversarial** — the goal is to catch errors and build shared understanding.
- Reviewing is a **contribution** to the project, and should be recognized as such.
- PIs/PMs: build the culture by **protecting time** for review.
:::

::: {.aside}
Full detail and the plan template: [Code review](../03-code/code-review.qmd).
:::

# The rest of the SOP {background-color="#2aa198"}

## Also worth knowing

::: {.notes}
Lighter touch on the other new code material — mention and point to the chapters.
:::

More new material in the Code section — skim when relevant:

::: {.incremental}
- **[Documentation](../03-code/code-documentation.qmd)** — comment the *why*; Roxygen2 for functions.
- **[Functional programming](../03-code/functional-programming.qmd)** — don't repeat yourself; `map`/`apply` over loops.
- **[Reproducibility](../03-code/index.qmd#reproducibility)** — [`renv`](../03-code/package-reproducibility.qmd) for packages, [`targets`](../03-code/pipeline-reproducibility.qmd) for pipelines.
:::

## Foundations you already know

::: {.notes}
The basic Git and Quarto material is largely familiar — a quick pointer is enough.
:::

Familiar ground, covered in full in the SOP:

::: {.incremental}
- **Basic Git** — [setup](../04-gh/01-setup.qmd), [commits](../04-gh/03-commits.qmd), and [Issues](../04-gh/04-issues.qmd).
- **Quarto** — [chunks](../05-quarto/chunk-best-practices.qmd), [figures & references](../05-quarto/figures-tables-references.qmd), and [presentations](../05-quarto/presentations.qmd) (like this one!).
:::

## Recap

::: {.notes}
Tie it back to the three new requirements.
:::

The updated SOP makes three things universal:

::: {.incremental .no-bullets}
- ✓ **Format on save** — Air for R, Ruff for Python.
- ✓ **Branch-based development** — never commit to `main`.
- ✓ **Code review through pull requests** — planned from day one.
:::

::: {.fragment}
All three serve the same two goals: **accuracy** and **reproducibility**.
:::

## Find everything in the SOP

The full Standard Operating Procedures live at:

[**emlab-ucsb.github.io/SOP**](https://emlab-ucsb.github.io/SOP/)

::: {.aside}
Source: [github.com/emlab-ucsb/SOP](https://github.com/emlab-ucsb/SOP)
:::

## Questions?
